card.indications: Management of pain severe enough to require an opioid when alternative treatments are inadequate. Some combination products are used for cough, but indications depend on the exact formulation.
card.mechanism: Weak mu-opioid receptor agonist that is partly converted by CYP2D6 to morphine, producing analgesia, sedation, respiratory depression, and reduced gastrointestinal motility.
card.dosing: Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Adult immediate-release dosing is individualized by pain severity, prior opioid exposure, age, organ function, and response. Avoid routine use in children because of unpredictable metabolism and life-threatening respiratory depression risk.
card.routeAdministration: Give exactly as prescribed. Measure liquids with an accurate device. Reassess pain, sedation, and respiratory rate before and after dosing. Taper rather than stopping abruptly after prolonged use.
card.contraindications: Significant respiratory depression, acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting, gastrointestinal obstruction, hypersensitivity, and use in children younger than 12 years. Avoid after tonsillectomy or adenoidectomy in patients younger than 18 years.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include addiction, abuse, misuse, life-threatening respiratory depression, accidental ingestion, neonatal opioid withdrawal, CYP3A4 interactions, and profound sedation or death with benzodiazepines or other CNS depressants.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, constipation, itching, dry mouth, and sweating.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory arrest, profound sedation, severe hypotension, anaphylaxis, seizures, serotonin syndrome, adrenal insufficiency, ileus, dependence, overdose, and death.
card.interactions: Benzodiazepines, alcohol, sedatives, gabapentinoids, and other opioids increase respiratory depression. MAO inhibitors are generally avoided within 14 days. CYP3A4 and CYP2D6 modifiers can alter exposure.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain relief, respiratory rate, oxygenation, sedation, blood pressure, bowel function, fall risk, misuse risk, and withdrawal.
card.patientTeaching: Take only as prescribed, never share, and avoid alcohol or unapproved sedatives. Seek emergency help for slow breathing, extreme sleepiness, blue lips, or inability to awaken.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food to reduce nausea. Increase fluids and fiber unless contraindicated. Avoid alcohol completely.

card.indications: Treatment and prophylaxis of gout flares and treatment of familial Mediterranean fever, depending on product labeling.
card.mechanism: Binds tubulin and inhibits microtubule polymerization, reducing neutrophil migration and inflammatory activity. It does not lower uric acid.
card.dosing: Gout-flare treatment commonly uses 1.2 mg at the first sign followed by 0.6 mg one hour later. Prophylaxis and FMF regimens vary. Renal or hepatic impairment and interacting drugs may require major dose reduction or avoidance.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Do not repeat flare courses more often than prescribed. Verify kidney and liver function and interacting medications.
card.contraindications: Concomitant use of strong CYP3A4 or P-glycoprotein inhibitors in patients with renal or hepatic impairment. Hypersensitivity to colchicine.
card.warnings: Has a narrow therapeutic index. Fatal toxicity can occur with overdose or interacting drugs. Bone-marrow suppression, neuromuscular toxicity, rhabdomyolysis, and multiorgan failure are possible.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and cramping.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe diarrhea with dehydration, pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, aplastic anemia, myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, neuropathy, hepatic injury, renal failure, and fatal overdose.
card.interactions: Strong CYP3A4 or P-gp inhibitors can cause toxic accumulation. Statins and fibrates increase myopathy risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain and swelling, bowel symptoms, CBC, renal and hepatic function, muscle weakness or pain, and interacting medications.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as directed and do not exceed the dose. Stop and contact the prescriber for severe diarrhea or vomiting. Report muscle pain, weakness, unusual bruising, fever, or sore throat.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain hydration. Avoid grapefruit if advised.

card.indications: Adjunct to diet and exercise for reduction of LDL cholesterol in primary hyperlipidemia. Some products are also used to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes.
card.mechanism: Binds bile acids in the intestine and prevents reabsorption, increasing conversion of cholesterol to bile acids and lowering LDL cholesterol.
card.dosing: Common adult dosing is 3.75 g once daily or divided twice daily, depending on formulation. Take with meals and liquid.
card.routeAdministration: Take tablets with a meal and liquid. Mix powder thoroughly and drink promptly. Separate many oral medications by at least 4 hours before colesevelam.
card.contraindications: Serum triglycerides above 500 mg/dL, history of hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis, or bowel obstruction.
card.warnings: Can raise triglycerides and may cause pancreatitis. Can worsen constipation or cause bowel obstruction. May reduce absorption of fat-soluble vitamins and many medications.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Constipation, dyspepsia, nausea, abdominal pain, headache, and weakness.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Bowel obstruction, severe constipation, hypertriglyceridemia, pancreatitis, dysphagia, and choking with tablets.
card.interactions: Can reduce absorption of levothyroxine, warfarin, oral contraceptives, phenytoin, sulfonylureas, cyclosporine, and fat-soluble vitamins.
card.monitoring: Monitor lipid panel, triglycerides, bowel function, glucose and A1C when used for diabetes, and effectiveness of interacting medications.
card.patientTeaching: Take with meals and enough liquid. Do not swallow dry powder. Report severe abdominal pain, persistent constipation, vomiting, or difficulty swallowing.
card.nutrition: Continue the prescribed cholesterol-lowering and diabetes meal plan. Maintain fluids and fiber unless contraindicated.

card.indications: Prevention of asthma symptoms, allergic rhinitis, allergic conjunctivitis, and management of mastocytosis-related symptoms depending on formulation. It is not a rescue bronchodilator.
card.mechanism: Stabilizes mast cells and inhibits release of histamine and other inflammatory mediators.
card.dosing: Dosing depends on formulation and indication and is usually scheduled regularly. Asthma prevention may require multiple daily doses. Oral concentrate for mastocytosis is diluted before use.
card.routeAdministration: Use consistently, not only when symptoms occur. Inhaled cromolyn does not treat acute bronchospasm. Demonstrate correct nebulizer, nasal, eye-drop, or oral-dilution technique.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to cromolyn or formulation ingredients.
card.warnings: Acute bronchospasm and anaphylaxis are rare. Symptom control may take days to weeks. Do not abruptly replace corticosteroids or rescue therapy without prescriber direction.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Throat irritation, cough, unpleasant taste, nasal irritation, sneezing, eye stinging, headache, and nausea.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Bronchospasm, severe wheezing, angioedema, anaphylaxis, and marked local hypersensitivity.
card.interactions: Few clinically important systemic interactions because absorption is low. Continue other asthma or allergy therapies as directed.
card.monitoring: Assess symptom frequency, rescue-inhaler use, lung sounds, peak flow when used for asthma, local irritation, adherence, and correct device technique.
card.patientTeaching: Use every day as prescribed. Keep rescue medication available for acute asthma symptoms. Seek urgent care for severe wheezing, breathing difficulty, or facial swelling.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions for inhaled, nasal, or ophthalmic forms. Dilute oral concentrate exactly as directed.

card.indications: Short-term adjunct to rest and physical therapy for acute painful musculoskeletal spasm. It is not effective for spasticity caused by cerebral or spinal cord disease.
card.mechanism: Acts centrally in the brainstem to reduce tonic somatic motor activity, likely through serotonergic and noradrenergic pathways. It does not act directly on skeletal muscle.
card.dosing: Immediate-release dosing commonly starts at 5 mg three times daily and may increase if needed. Extended-release products are generally once daily. Use is usually limited to 2 to 3 weeks.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Swallow extended-release capsules whole; some products may be opened and sprinkled according to labeling.
card.contraindications: Use of MAO inhibitors within 14 days, acute recovery after myocardial infarction, arrhythmias, heart block, conduction disturbances, congestive heart failure, or hyperthyroidism.
card.warnings: Can cause marked sedation, anticholinergic effects, falls, and serotonin syndrome. Structurally related to tricyclic antidepressants and can affect cardiac conduction.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dry mouth, dizziness, fatigue, constipation, blurred vision, and nausea.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Serotonin syndrome, arrhythmias, severe hypotension, seizures, confusion, urinary retention, and serious allergic reactions.
card.interactions: Alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, antihistamines, and other CNS depressants increase sedation. Serotonergic drugs increase serotonin-syndrome risk. MAO inhibitors are contraindicated.
card.monitoring: Assess pain and spasm, sedation, gait, falls, bowel and bladder function, heart rate, rhythm symptoms, and signs of serotonin syndrome.
card.patientTeaching: Use only for the prescribed short period. Avoid alcohol and driving until effects are known. Report palpitations, fainting, severe agitation, fever, sweating, tremor, or muscle rigidity.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food if stomach upset occurs. Maintain fluids and fiber. Avoid alcohol.
